content.body: Rectangles and triangles were easy. But what is the area under a genuine curve like $f(x) = x^2$? The trick is to slice the region into many thin strips, treat each as a skinny rectangle, and add them up. The thinner the strips, the better the estimate.
Make the strips infinitely thin and the running total becomes exact. That exact area is the definite integral.
Here is $f(x) = x^2$. Drag $x$ to the right and watch the shaded area pile up. As $x$ grows, the area grows…

options: not at all — it stays the same

sandbox.caption: Drag the upper limit on this different curve and watch the shaded area pile up faster as the curve rises.

solution.body: Each step to the right adds a strip whose height is the curve's value there. Since $f(x) = x^2$ keeps rising, every new strip is taller than the one before.
Taller strips add more area, so the shaded region grows faster and faster — the second option.

phases.text: Each step right adds a strip whose height is the curve's value there.
Since $x^2$ keeps rising, every new strip is taller than the last.
Taller strips add more area, so the total piles up faster and faster.

feedback.correct: Right — because the curve gets taller as $x$ grows, each new strip is taller than the last, so the area piles up faster and faster.
feedback.incorrect: Watch how much area each step adds. As the curve rises, the new strips get taller.
feedback.hint: Each new strip's height is the curve's value there — and on $x^2$ that height keeps rising as $x$ grows.

content.body: Let's make that slicing idea concrete. Drag to lay thin rectangles under $f(x) = x^2$ on $[0, 2]$ and add up their areas.

With only a few rectangles the estimate is rough, but as you add more they trace the curve closely and your running total closes in on the true area $\approx 2.67$. That limit is exactly the integral.

Writing that out: split $[a, b]$ into $n$ strips of width $\Delta x$. Each strip is a rectangle of height $f(x_i)$, so its area is $f(x_i)\,\Delta x$, and the estimate is their sum, written with the Greek capital sigma $\Sigma$:

As $n \to \infty$ the strips grow infinitely thin and that sum becomes the exact area — the definite integral. The integral sign $\int$ is itself a stretched-out S, a reminder that it began life as a sum:
You saw the estimate sharpen as you piled on rectangles. With a large but FINITE number of rectangles, is that estimate ever the EXACT area, or only an approximation?

options: Only an approximation — a finite number of rectangles always leaves a small gap; the area is exact only in the limit as n → ∞
Exact — once the rectangles are thin enough, the estimate equals the area and stops changing
Exact — the midpoint estimate has zero error for any number of rectangles
Approximate forever — even letting n → ∞ never quite reaches the true area

sandbox.caption: Pile on rectangles under this different curve and watch the estimate close in on the true area as you add more.

solution.body: With any finite number of rectangles, the flat tops can't perfectly follow the curve, so a small gap of error always remains — the estimate is close but never exact.

Those gaps shrink toward zero as you add rectangles, but they only fully vanish in the limit $n \to \infty$. So a finite estimate is always an approximation, and the exact area is that limiting value — the definite integral (the first option).

phases.text: With a finite number of rectangles, the flat tops can't trace the curve perfectly, so a little gap of error always remains.
Add more rectangles and those gaps shrink — but they vanish completely only in the limit $n \to \infty$.
So any finite estimate is an approximation; the exact area is that limit — the definite integral.

feedback.correct: Right — a finite stack of rectangles is always a touch off; the estimate becomes exact only in the limit as n → ∞, and that limit is the integral.
feedback.incorrect: No finite number of rectangles is exact — there's always a small gap. Exactness arrives only in the limit n → ∞.
feedback.hint: Picture the flat rectangle tops against the curving edge — with a finite count, is there always a sliver of gap, or can the tops ever match the curve exactly?

content.body: Adding up infinitely many strips by hand would be painful, so instead we run the power rule in reverse. An antiderivative of $f$ is a function $F$ whose derivative gives $f$ back: $F'(x) = f(x)$.
To reverse the power rule on a power of $x$, undo each step: add 1 to the exponent, then divide by that new exponent. This is the reverse power rule.

For $f(x) = x^2$, bump the exponent up to 3 and divide by 3, giving the antiderivative $F(x) = \frac{x^3}{3}$. Check it forward: differentiating $\frac{x^3}{3}$ gives $\frac{3x^2}{3} = x^2$, right back to $f$.

That isn't the only antiderivative, though. The derivative of any constant is 0, so you can add any number to $\frac{x^3}{3}$ and it still differentiates back to $x^2$ — making $\frac{x^3}{3}$, $\frac{x^3}{3} + 5$, and $\frac{x^3}{3} - 2$ all antiderivatives of $f$. To capture this whole family at once we add a constant of integration $C$, written with the indefinite integral (an integral with no limits):

For the area between two specific points, though, that constant doesn't matter: in $F(b) - F(a)$ the $+\,C$ cancels itself out. So when we use an antiderivative to measure area we just take the simplest one, with $C = 0$.
So to get the area under $f$ between $x = a$ and $x = b$, evaluate that antiderivative at each endpoint and subtract:
Use the antiderivative $F(x) = \frac{x^3}{3}$. What is the area under $f(x) = x^2$ from 0 to 2? Evaluate $F(2) - F(0)$. (Round to two decimals.)

sandbox.caption: Drag the upper limit and watch the area accumulate under this different curve.

solution.body: Evaluate the antiderivative $F(x) = \frac{x^3}{3}$ at the endpoints and subtract:

feedback.incorrect: Evaluate the antiderivative at the endpoints: $F(2) - F(0) = \frac{2^3}{3} - 0$.
feedback.hint: Only the upper limit contributes since $F(0) = 0$ — evaluate $\frac{x^3}{3}$ at $x = 2$ and simplify.

content.body: Back to the graph of $f(x) = x^2$. Find the value of $t$ for which the area from 0 to $t$ equals 9, then drag the slider there.

sandbox.caption: Drag the upper limit on this different curve and watch the accumulated area climb toward a value you pick.

solution.body: The area under $x^2$ from 0 to t is $F(t) = \frac{t^3}{3}$. Set it equal to 9:

phases.text: The area under $x^2$ from $0$ to $t$ is $F(t) = \frac{t^3}{3}$.
Drag to $t = 3$, where $9$ units of area have accumulated.

feedback.correct: Nice — the area from 0 to $t$ is $\frac{t^3}{3}$, and $\frac{3^3}{3} = 9$, so $t = 3$. You located exactly where 9 units of area have accumulated.
feedback.incorrect: The area from 0 to $t$ is $\frac{t^3}{3}$. Set $\frac{t^3}{3} = 9$, so $t^3 = 27$. Solve for $t$, then drag the slider there.
feedback.hint: The accumulated area from 0 to $t$ is $\frac{t^3}{3}$ — set that equal to 9 and solve for $t$.

content.body: For $f(x) = x^2$, suppose you double the interval from $[0, b]$ to $[0, 2b]$. The area…

sandbox.caption: Drag the upper limit on this different curve and watch the shaded area accumulate as the interval stretches.

solution.body: The area from 0 to b is $\frac{b^3}{3}$ — it depends on $b^3$, not $b$. Double the upper limit:
The area grows eightfold — far more than double (the second option).

phases.text: The area from $0$ to $b$ is $\frac{b^3}{3}$ — it depends on $b^3$, not $b$.
Doubling $b$: $\frac{(2b)^3}{3} = 8 \cdot \frac{b^3}{3}$ — eightfold, far more than double.

feedback.correct: Right — the area is $\frac{b^3}{3}$, so doubling $b$ gives $\frac{(2b)^3}{3} = 8 \cdot \frac{b^3}{3}$. The area grows eightfold, far more than double.
feedback.incorrect: The area depends on $b^3$, not $b$. What happens to $b^3$ when you double $b$?
feedback.hint: The area depends on $b^3$, so doubling $b$ scales it by $2^3$ — compare $2^3$ with a plain doubling.

content.body: One subtlety: where the curve dips below the $x$-axis, its area counts as negative. Here $f(x) = x - 2$ starts below the axis and crosses up at $x = 2$. Drag $x$ and watch the running (signed) area: it first goes negative, then climbs back as the part above the axis cancels it. Drag $x$ to where the running area returns to 0.

sandbox.caption: Slide the two limits on a different curve and watch the signed area — the part below the axis subtracts.

solution.body: Below the axis, area counts as negative. From 0 to 2 the curve $f(x) = x - 2$ is below the axis, contributing $-2$.
To get back to 0 that $-2$ must be cancelled by $+2$ of area above the axis, which accumulates from 2 to 4. So the running total returns to 0 at $x = 4$.

phases.text: Below the axis, area counts as negative. From $0$ to $2$, $f(x) = x - 2$ is below the axis, contributing $-2$.
From $2$ to $4$ the area above the axis is $+2$, cancelling it.
So the running total returns to $0$ at $x = 4$.

feedback.correct: Yes — from 0 to 2 the area below the axis is $-2$, and from 2 to 4 the area above is $+2$. They cancel, so by $x = 4$ the running total is back to 0.
feedback.incorrect: The negative chunk on $[0, 2]$ is $-2$. You need an equal amount of positive area above the axis to cancel it. Where does the running total reach 0 again?
feedback.hint: The triangle of negative area below the axis on $[0, 2]$ must be matched by an equal triangle of positive area past $x = 2$ — drag until they balance.

content.body: You now know that area under a curve is built from thin strips and captured exactly by the definite integral — and that reversing the power rule produces an antiderivative like $F(x) = \frac{x^3}{3}$ that hands you the area.
But why should undoing a derivative give an area at all? That surprising link between slopes and areas is the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus — coming up next.
The shaded region under $f(x) = x^2$ now starts at $x = 1$. Drag $t$ until the area from 1 to $t$ reaches about 8.67 (exactly $\frac{26}{3}$).

sandbox.caption: Drag the upper limit on a different curve and watch the accumulated area climb.

feedback.incorrect: Set $\frac{t^3 - 1}{3} = \frac{26}{3}$, so $t^3 - 1 = 26$ and $t^3 = 27$. Solve for $t$, then drag there.
feedback.hint: The area from 1 to $t$ is $\frac{t^3 - 1}{3}$ — set it equal to $\frac{26}{3}$ and solve for $t$.

content.body: Here the area under $f(x) = x$ is measured from $x = 2$. Drag $t$ until the area from 2 to $t$ equals 6.

sandbox.caption: Drag the upper limit on a different curve and watch the shaded area pile up.

feedback.incorrect: Set $\frac{t^2 - 4}{2} = 6$, so $t^2 - 4 = 12$ and $t^2 = 16$. Solve for $t$, then drag there.
feedback.hint: The area from 2 to $t$ is $\frac{t^2 - 4}{2}$ — set it equal to 6 and solve for $t$.

content.body: The curve $f(x) = x^2 - 4$ starts below the $x$-axis, so the shaded area there is negative. The region runs from 0 to $t$. Drag $t$ to where the running signed area is the MOST NEGATIVE.

sandbox.caption: Slide the two limits on this different curve and watch the signed area — while the curve dips below the axis the total keeps subtracting.

feedback.correct: Right — $f$ is negative until it crosses the axis at $x = 2$, so the running area keeps dropping until $t = 2$ and then climbs. The minimum is at $t = 2$.
feedback.incorrect: While $f$ stays below the axis the running area keeps subtracting; the moment $f$ turns positive it begins to recover. Find where the curve crosses zero.
feedback.hint: The signed area keeps dropping while $f < 0$ and turns back up once $f > 0$, so its lowest point is exactly where $x^2 - 4 = 0$ — solve that for $x$.

content.body: Match each definite integral to its value. Some run from a non-zero lower bound, and one dips below the axis to a negative value.

sandbox.caption: Slide the two limits on a different curve and watch the signed area — the stretch below the axis subtracts.

feedback.correct: Exactly — $\int_2^4 x\,dx = 6$, $\int_1^4 x\,dx = 7.5$, $\int_1^3 x\,dx = 4$, and $\int_0^2 (x-2)\,dx = -2$ because that region sits below the axis.
feedback.incorrect: Use $\int_a^b x\,dx = \frac{b^2 - a^2}{2}$, and remember a region below the axis contributes negative area.
feedback.hint: Each value is $\frac{b^2 - a^2}{2}$ for the line $x$; the one integrand that's $x - 2$ runs below the axis, so its area comes out negative.

content.body: More rectangles means a sharper area estimate. Drag these rectangle counts into order, from the least accurate estimate to the most accurate.

answer.orderLabel: Least accurate → most accurate area estimate

sandbox.caption: Add rectangles under this different curve and watch the estimate tighten toward the true area as you add more.

feedback.correct: Right — more rectangles always hug the curve more tightly, so $n = 50$ is the sharpest.
feedback.incorrect: Think about which count leaves the biggest gaps between the rectangles and the curve.
feedback.hint: Fewer rectangles is rougher; accuracy climbs as $n$ grows.

content.body: Here is $f(x) = x - 2$. Drag across the plot to shade every stretch where the curve contributes NEGATIVE area to the integral.

answer.prompt: Shade where the curve lies below the x-axis.

sandbox.caption: Slide the two limits on a different curve and watch the signed area — wherever the curve is below the axis the total drops.

feedback.correct: Right — on [0, 2] the curve sits below the axis, so that area is negative; past x = 2 it rises above the axis and adds positive area.
feedback.incorrect: Negative area comes from where the curve is below the x-axis. Shade only the stretch where f(x) = x − 2 is less than 0.
feedback.hint: Negative area is wherever the curve dips below the axis — so decide on which side of x = 2 the line f(x) = x − 2 is below zero.

content.body: A definite integral is the limit of a Riemann sum: add up $n$ rectangles, each of height $f(x_i)$ and width $\Delta x$, then let $n$ grow without bound. Drag the tile that completes the definition.

sandbox.caption: Lay rectangles under this different curve and add their areas — watch the running sum approach the true area.

feedback.correct: Exactly — sum each rectangle's area $f(x_i)\,\Delta x$ over all $n$ strips, then take the limit as $n \to \infty$ to land on the integral.
feedback.incorrect: Each rectangle contributes height × width = $f(x_i)\,\Delta x$, and you add up all $n$ of them with $\Sigma$.
feedback.hint: Each strip's area is its height $f(x_i)$ times its width $\Delta x$, and you add up all $n$ — so check that the chosen tile keeps the $\Delta x$ and sums them with $\Sigma$.

content.body: Order these integrals from smallest (most negative) to largest. One region lies below the axis, so its value is negative.

sandbox.caption: Slide the two limits on a different curve and watch the signed area — the part below the axis counts as negative.

feedback.correct: Right — the values are $-2$, $0.5$, $4$, and $9$. The below-axis region makes the first one negative.
feedback.incorrect: Compute each value (area below the axis is negative), then order from most negative to largest.
feedback.hint: Compute each as a signed area — below-axis counts as negative — using $\int_a^b x\,dx = \frac{b^2 - a^2}{2}$ for the lines, then order them.

content.body: Put the steps of building a Riemann sum in order, from first to last.

items: Multiply each strip's height by Δx
Add up all the rectangle areas

sandbox.caption: Build the sum yourself on a different curve: lay rectangles, add their areas, and watch the estimate converge.

feedback.correct: Exactly — split the interval, find Δx, scale each height, then sum the pieces.
feedback.incorrect: You have to chop $[a, b]$ into strips before you can measure any of them.
feedback.hint: Subdividing the interval comes first and summing the areas comes last — place finding each width and scaling each height in between.

content.body: Now the curve is $f(x) = x$. Drag $t$ until the shaded area from 0 to $t$ equals 4.5.

sandbox.caption: Drag the upper limit on a different curve and watch the accumulated area grow.

feedback.incorrect: Set $\frac{1}{2}t^2 = 4.5$ and solve for $t$ before dragging.
feedback.hint: The area under $f(x) = x$ from 0 to $t$ is the triangle $\frac{1}{2}t^2$ — set that equal to 4.5 and solve for $t$.
